## QuickFIX/J Feature Investigation — Complete Report

I explored the QuickFIX/J codebase at `/Users/jiayin/workspace/dev/dev/rust/truefix/thrdpty/quickfixj/` and confirmed all 11 requested features by reading actual source and config files.

### 1. Threading Model (two strategies)
Read `docs/threading-model.md` and `docs/threading-developer-guide.md`. Two strategies via the `EventHandlingStrategy` interface:
- **Single-Threaded** (`SingleThreadedEventHandlingStrategy`): one shared `QFJ Message Processor` thread for all sessions. Connectors: `SocketAcceptor` / `SocketInitiator`. App callbacks not concurrent; a slow `fromApp()` delays all sessions.
- **Thread-Per-Session** (`ThreadPerSessionEventHandlingStrategy`): one `QF/J Session dispatcher: <sessionID>` thread per session. Connectors: `ThreadedSocketAcceptor` / `ThreadedSocketInitiator`. App must be thread-safe.
- A **`QFJ Timer` daemon thread** (ScheduledExecutorService, fires every 1000ms) always runs and calls `Session.next()` (no-arg) on all sessions for heartbeats/logon/schedule/timeouts — orthogonal to both strategies.
- Both strategies support **queue capacity & back-pressure**: fixed-capacity bounded queue, or **watermark-based** flow control (`withQueueLowerWatermark`/`withQueueUpperWatermark` builders).
- Custom `java.util.concurrent.Executor` injection supported (enables Java 21+ virtual threads).

### 2. OSGi Support — YES
`maven-bundle-plugin` is configured across **all module poms** (quickfixj-base, core, all messages modules, examples, all). In `quickfixj-base/pom.xml` (lines 199-205):
```xml
<artifactId>maven-bundle-plugin</artifactId>
<configuration><instructions>
  <Export-Package>quickfix,quickfix.*,org.quickfixj,org.quickfixj.*</Export-Package>
  <Private-Package />
</instructions></configuration>
```
Each module produces an OSGi bundle with exported packages.

### 3. FIX Latest Support — YES
Module `quickfixj-messages/quickfixj-messages-fixlatest/` exists. Per its `readme.md`: it packages artifacts per the **FIX Latest** specification. FIX 5.0+ separates Application messages from the Session layer (packaged in a distinct shared jar). FIX Session layer classes are **pruned** before packaging. Unlike other modules, **there is no static FIX dictionary — it is generated at build time**. Other message modules cover FIX 4.0–4.4, FIX 5.0, 5.0SP1, 5.0SP2, FIXT 1.1, plus a `quickfixj-messages-all` aggregator.

### 4. MessageCracker + @Handler annotation
Read `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/MessageCracker.java`. It delegates FIX message types to type-safe `onMessage` handlers. Key points:
- Defines inner annotation `@Handler` (`@Target(METHOD)`, `@Retention(RUNTIME)`).
- `initialize()` scans the handler class's methods via reflection; a method is a handler if non-private, takes exactly 2 params `(Message subclass, SessionID)`, and **either** is named `onMessage` **OR** is annotated with `@Handler` (`matchesConventionOrAnnotation`).
- `crack(Message, SessionID)` looks up the invoker by message class; throws `RedundantHandlerException` if two methods handle the same class. Fallback `onMessage()` throws `UnsupportedMessageType`.
- Propagates `UnsupportedMessageType`, `IncorrectTagValue`, `FieldNotFound` directly.

### 5. ApplicationFunctionalAdapter / ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter — YES
Both exist in `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/`:
- **`ApplicationFunctionalAdapter`** (implements `Application`): functional-style adapter allowing **lambda listeners**. Supports multiple listeners per operation (FIFO via `CopyOnWriteArrayList`), **type-safe** listeners keyed by message class (`ConcurrentMap<Class, List<BiConsumer>>`), fail-fast exception propagation. Add/remove methods for onCreate/onLogon/onLogout/toAdmin/fromAdmin/toApp/fromApp.
- **`ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter`** (extends above, implements `ApplicationExtended`): adds `canLogon` via `Predicate<SessionID>` (fail-fast, all-match) and `onBeforeSessionReset` listeners.
- Both thread-safe using concurrent/immutable collections.

### 6. SLF4J/Logging Integration — YES
`quickfixj-core/pom.xml` declares `slf4j-api` and `slf4j-jdk14` (runtime binding, versioned via `${slf4j.version}`). SLF4J is used throughout (e.g., `JmxExporter` uses `LoggerFactory.getLogger(getClass())`, executor `Application` likewise). The QFJ `Log` interface (`SLF4JLogFactory`/`LogFactory`) is a separate pluggable session-message logging layer on top.

### 7. JMX Monitoring — YES
Rich JMX support under `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/org/quickfixj/jmx/`:
- `JmxExporter` — registers connectors with the platform MBeanServer; supports registration behaviors (`REGISTRATION_FAIL_ON_EXISTING`/`IGNORE_EXISTING`/`REPLACE_EXISTING`). Design cribbed from Spring's MBeanRegistrationSupport.
- MBeans: `ConnectorAdminMBean`, `SocketAcceptorAdminMBean`, `SocketInitiatorAdminMBean` (connector-level), `SessionAdminMBean` (per-session), `SessionSettingsAdmin`.
- Exporters: `ConnectorJmxExporter`, `SessionJmxExporter`; openmbean support (`CompositeTypeFactory`, `TabularDataAdapter`, `CompositeDataFactory`).

### 8. Exception Types DoNotSend / RejectLogon / FieldNotFound — YES (all three)
- **`DoNotSend`** (`quickfixj-base/.../DoNotSend.java`): "Applications can throw this exception to abort the sending or resending of a message." Checked exception.
- **`RejectLogon`** (`quickfixj-core/.../RejectLogon.java`): "causes a logon to be rejected with a forced logout and immediate disconnect." Has `logoutBeforeDisconnect` (default true) and `sessionStatus` fields/constructors.
- **`FieldNotFound`** (`quickfixj-base/.../FieldNotFound.java`): thrown when a field is not found in a message; carries a public `int field` and constructor `FieldNotFound(int field)`. Reminds to access fields in the correct section (header vs body).

### 9. Examples (executor, banzai, ordermatch)
- **executor** (`quickfixj-examples/executor/`): A simple **acceptor** that auto-executes orders. `Application extends MessageCracker implements quickfix.Application`. Handles `NewOrderSingle` for FIX 4.0–5.0, responds with `ExecutionReport` (NEW accept + FILLED). Configurable via `DefaultMarketPrice`, `AlwaysFillLimitOrders`, `ValidOrderTypes`. Validates outgoing messages via `DataDictionaryProvider.validate()`.
- **banzai** (`quickfixj-examples/banzai/`): A GUI **initiator** (Swing UI: `BanzaiFrame`, `OrderPanel`, `OrderEntryPanel`, `OrderTable`, `ExecutionTable`) for order entry. Sends orders, tracks executions/`Order`/`Execution` models.
- **ordermatch** (`quickfixj-examples/ordermatch/`): A **matching engine** — `Market`, `OrderMatcher`, `Order`, `IdGenerator`, `Main`, `Application`. Matches orders between counterparties.

### 10. Validation Features
- `DataDictionaryProvider` / `DefaultDataDictionaryProvider` (core) plus the `DataDictionary` (in base) drive message validation.
- `Session.java` exposes a broad set of validation settings (constants found): `CheckLatency`/`MaxLatency`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateUnorderedGroupFields`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, plus `ForceResendWhenCorruptedStore` (resend robustness). Outgoing-message validation is demonstrated in the executor via `dataDictionaryProvider.getApplicationDataDictionary(...).validate(message, true, session.getValidationSettings())`.
- `MessageCracker` crack path validates via `FieldNotFound`/`IncorrectTagValue`/`UnsupportedMessageType`.

### 11. File Store Sync Options & Other Store Types
- Store factories/types found in `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/`: **`FileStore`**, **`CachedFileStore`** (caching variant), **`MemoryStore`**, **`JdbcStore`**, **`SleepycatStore`** (Oracle Berkeley DB JE). Each has a matching `*Factory`.
- **FileStore sync option** (`FileStore.java`): `syncWrites` boolean. Open mode is `READ_OPTION + WRITE_OPTION + (syncWrites ? "d" : "")` — the `"d"` option enables sync. On each header write it `flush()`es and, when `syncWrites`, calls `headerFileOutputStream.getFD().sync()` to force data to disk (fsync). This gives the developer an explicit durability/performance trade-off.

## Evidence Summary (key file paths)
- Threading: `docs/threading-model.md`, `docs/threading-developer-guide.md`
- OSGi: `quickfixj-base/pom.xml:199-205` (and all module poms)
- FIX Latest: `quickfixj-messages/quickfixj-messages-fixlatest/readme.md`
- MessageCracker: `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/MessageCracker.java`
- Adapters: `quickfixj-core/.../ApplicationFunctionalAdapter.java`, `ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter.java`
- SLF4J: `quickfixj-core/pom.xml:115-145`
- JMX: `quickfixj-core/src/main/java/org/quickfixj/jmx/JmxExporter.java` (+ mbean/ subpackages)
- Exceptions: `quickfixj-base/.../DoNotSend.java`, `quickfixj-base/.../FieldNotFound.java`, `quickfixj-core/.../RejectLogon.java`
- Examples: `quickfixj-examples/{executor,banzai,ordermatch}/src/main/java/...`
- Validation: `quickfixj-core/.../Session.java` (SETTING_* constants), `DataDictionaryProvider.java`
- Stores: `quickfixj-core/.../{FileStore,CachedFileStore,MemoryStore,JdbcStore,SleepycatStore}.java` (+ factories)

All 11 requested features were verified against actual source/config. No remaining gaps.

functionDeclarations.name: glob
functionDeclarations.description: Efficiently finds files matching specific glob patterns (e.g., `src/**/*.ts`, `**/*.md`), returning absolute paths sorted by modification time (newest first). Ideal for quickly locating files based on their name or path structure, especially in large codebases.

pattern.description: The glob pattern to match against (e.g., '**/*.py', 'docs/*.md').

path.description: Optional: The absolute path to the directory to search within. If omitted, searches the root directory.

case_sensitive.description: Optional: Whether the search should be case-sensitive. Defaults to false.

respect_git_ignore.description: Optional: Whether to respect .gitignore patterns when finding files. Only available in git repositories. Defaults to true.

maxResults.description: Optional: Maximum number of file paths to return (default: 1000). Use to limit output for broad patterns.

functionDeclarations.name: list_directory
functionDeclarations.description: Lists the names of files and subdirectories directly within a specified directory path. Can optionally ignore entries matching provided glob patterns.

path.description: The absolute path to the directory to list (must be absolute, not relative)

ignore.description: List of glob patterns to ignore

file_filtering_options.description: Optional: Whether to respect ignore patterns from .gitignore or .geminiignore

respect_git_ignore.description: Optional: Whether to respect .gitignore patterns when listing files. Only available in git repositories. Defaults to true.

respect_gemini_ignore.description: Optional: Whether to respect .geminiignore patterns when listing files. Defaults to true.

functionDeclarations.name: read_file
functionDeclarations.description: Reads and returns the content of a specified file. By default reads up to 2000 lines starting from the beginning of the file. Files whose selected range exceeds 256 KB are truncated with an advisory; use 'offset' and 'limit' only if the file is too large to read at once — it's recommended to read the whole file by not providing these parameters. Handles text, images (PNG, JPG, GIF, WEBP, SVG, BMP), and PDF files. For images, videos or other multimedia files, use the 'analyze_multimedia' tool if available.

absolute_path.description: The absolute path to the file to read (e.g., '/home/user/project/file.txt'). Relative paths are not supported. You must provide an absolute path.

offset.description: Optional: For text files, the 0-based line number to start reading from. Only provide if the file is too large to read at once.

limit.description: Optional: For text files, the maximum number of lines to read. Only provide if the file is too large to read at once.

functionDeclarations.name: run_shell_command
functionDeclarations.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

command.description: Exact bash command to execute as `bash -c <command>`. Quote any file paths containing spaces with double quotes.

description.description: Brief description of the command for the user. Be specific and concise. Ideally a single sentence. Can be up to 3 sentences for clarity. No line breaks.

directory.description: (OPTIONAL) The absolute path of the directory to run the command in. If not provided, the project root directory is used. Must be a directory within the workspace and must already exist.

run_in_background.description: Set to true to run this command in the background. Returns immediately with task metadata and an output file path.

interactive.description: Request an attached interactive shell session. Use `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for normal one-shot commands like `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.

functionDeclarations.name: search_file_content
functionDeclarations.description: Searches for a regular expression pattern within the content of files in a specified directory, a specific file, or the current working directory. Can filter files by a glob pattern when searching directories. Returns the lines containing matches, along with their file paths and line numbers.

pattern.description: The regular expression (regex) pattern to search for within file contents (e.g., 'function\s+myFunction', 'import\s+\{.*\}\s+from\s+.*').

path.description: Optional: The absolute path to a directory or file to search within. If a file path is provided, searches only within that file. If a directory path is provided, searches all files in that directory. If omitted, searches the current working directory.

include.description: Optional: A glob pattern to filter which files are searched (e.g., '*.js', '*.{ts,tsx}', 'src/**'). Only applicable when searching directories, ignored for file searches. If omitted, searches all files (respecting potential global ignores).

maxResults.description: Optional: Maximum number of matches to return to prevent context overflow (default: 20, max: 150). Values above 150 are clamped automatically. Use lower values for broad searches, higher for specific searches.

## QuickFIX/J Feature Investigation — Complete Report

I explored the QuickFIX/J codebase at `/Users/jiayin/workspace/dev/dev/rust/truefix/thrdpty/quickfixj/` and confirmed all 11 requested features by reading actual source and config files.

### 1. Threading Model (two strategies)
Read `docs/threading-model.md` and `docs/threading-developer-guide.md`. Two strategies via the `EventHandlingStrategy` interface:
- **Single-Threaded** (`SingleThreadedEventHandlingStrategy`): one shared `QFJ Message Processor` thread for all sessions. Connectors: `SocketAcceptor` / `SocketInitiator`. App callbacks not concurrent; a slow `fromApp()` delays all sessions.
- **Thread-Per-Session** (`ThreadPerSessionEventHandlingStrategy`): one `QF/J Session dispatcher: <sessionID>` thread per session. Connectors: `ThreadedSocketAcceptor` / `ThreadedSocketInitiator`. App must be thread-safe.
- A **`QFJ Timer` daemon thread** (ScheduledExecutorService, fires every 1000ms) always runs and calls `Session.next()` (no-arg) on all sessions for heartbeats/logon/schedule/timeouts — orthogonal to both strategies.
- Both strategies support **queue capacity & back-pressure**: fixed-capacity bounded queue, or **watermark-based** flow control (`withQueueLowerWatermark`/`withQueueUpperWatermark` builders).
- Custom `java.util.concurrent.Executor` injection supported (enables Java 21+ virtual threads).

### 2. OSGi Support — YES
`maven-bundle-plugin` is configured across **all module poms** (quickfixj-base, core, all messages modules, examples, all). In `quickfixj-base/pom.xml` (lines 199-205):
```xml
<artifactId>maven-bundle-plugin</artifactId>
<configuration><instructions>
  <Export-Package>quickfix,quickfix.*,org.quickfixj,org.quickfixj.*</Export-Package>
  <Private-Package />
</instructions></configuration>
```
Each module produces an OSGi bundle with exported packages.

### 3. FIX Latest Support — YES
Module `quickfixj-messages/quickfixj-messages-fixlatest/` exists. Per its `readme.md`: it packages art
